title: Pomodoro Technique
source: The Pomodoro Technique by Francesco Cirillo
content:
The Pomodoro Technique is a time management method that uses timed work intervals (pomodoros) separated by short breaks. It was developed by Francesco Cirillo in the late 1980s, named after a tomato-shaped kitchen timer.

Core cycle:
1. Choose one task to focus on
2. Set a timer for 25 minutes (one pomodoro)
3. Work on the task with full focus until the timer rings
4. Take a 5-minute break
5. Every 4 pomodoros, take a longer break of 15-30 minutes

Why it works:
- Time boxing creates urgency that combats perfectionism and procrastination
- Frequent breaks prevent mental fatigue and maintain concentration quality
- The short intervals make large tasks feel approachable
- Tracking pomodoros gives a sense of accomplishment and concrete productivity data

Handling interruptions:
- Internal interruptions (your own impulses): Write the thought down on a 'to-do later' list and immediately return to the pomodoro. This is called 'inform, negotiate, schedule, call back.'
- External interruptions: If unavoidable, void the pomodoro and restart after handling the interruption. A pomodoro cannot be split.

Advanced usage:
- Estimate tasks in pomodoros before starting (3-5 pomodoros for complex tasks)
- Record actual vs. estimated pomodoros — this improves future estimation accuracy
- Use overlearning: if you finish early, review or improve your work rather than stopping

Common modifications:
- 50/10 pattern: 50 minutes of work, 10 minutes break (better for experienced practitioners)
- Deep Work sessions: 90 minutes of work, 15-20 minutes break (aligned with ultradian rhythms)
- The standard 25/5 is best for tasks prone to distraction or creative blocks

Integration with focus tracking: Count your daily pomodoros. Research suggests knowledge workers can sustain 8-10 pomodoros per day at high quality. Going above 12 typically leads to diminishing returns and next-day fatigue.
